Welcome to module 8, ongoing optimization and scale of the traffic engine. This module is unusual in that it's in a presentation format. It's really a conversational format is how I think about it. I've tried to write this module more times than I care to count and I've written so many versions of it. It never really got there. And I think the reason for that is that this module really, there's a lot of ways that scale and optimization at the high level can go wrong. There's a lot of quicksand to avoid. And the way to avoid that is to really understand how to think about scale and how to think about optimization. So what I hope to do, I'm going to use the same metaphor for the entirety of this presentation. And what I hope to do is by using that metaphor really help you have an understanding, really to see the world of scale through with paid traffic through the way that I see it. Now I can't guarantee that's the only way or it's capital T truth. It's the way that's informed for me for a couple of decades plus of experience. And I think there's value in it. And I want to share that value with you so that you can see the world really through my eyes as it comes as it applies to paid traffic. So let's jump in. The metaphor we're going to use here is a target bullseye. I mean, it'll make more sense later when I talk about the origin of this metaphor and who's responsible for it. I don't want to do that twice. So for now, I'll just, we'll just have this metaphor and then you'll see how it makes sense over the entirety of this presentation. So when we think about optimization and scale, I want you to think like an investor. That's really the best framework that I've come up with over the past couple of decades about what paid traffic is all about. So the first thing when you're an investor that you have to know is you have to know your goals with precision and clarity. You have to know what it is you're trying to accomplish. So for us using paid traffic, we need to know, and this is really what I described in the free paid traffic program about will it make the boat go faster? We have to know what the boat is. We know we have to know what faster means. If you have muddy goals, and this is common, I can't tell you how common this is. I've seen so many businesses where when I ask the question, what is it that we're trying to accomplish here so that it helps me make sure that I was doing my work well, the answer usually is some form of more or better. Those are not goals with precision and clarity, more or better relative to what? The easiest answer to this question, and it's one that Andre and I both embrace, and we in many ways hope you'll embrace it too, is that the goal of your business is to generate happy customers. So when you scale your business, the precision around and the clarity around the definition of what we're talking about for a goal is that you're trying to get more happy customers. It may be that you're retaining customers for a longer period of time that would contribute to their happiness. It will be specific to your business, specific to your business model, but what matters is that you have a very clear goal so that if you know today, you've invested $10,000 in paid traffic, and over the course of 30 days, you're going to get that $10,000 back with initial purchases. So first one CPA, second one's AOV, you're going to spend $10,000, you're going to get $10,000, and then over the life of that customer, they're going to stay for a long period of time, they're going to love what you do, and you're going to have some super fans, you're going to have fans, you're going to have happy customers, and when the number of those happy customers over time is increasing, then you know you're meeting this goal. It's clear, it's understandable, but if you just say, well, I want more, I want things to be better. It's so difficult to scale that, it's so difficult to optimize for more or better unless we get very clear about what more and better means relative to some point in time. The next way to think about, when I say think about, think like an investor, campaigns are investments that generate returns. This is, in many ways, this is incredibly obvious, yet at the same time, it's rare when you hear somebody think out loud and they express it this way, which is strange to me, because we're not spending money for paid traffic because we feel like spending money. We're putting money in, hoping that we get a positive return out. If you have an e-commerce business, then you're probably using ROAS to calculate returns, return on ad spend. When you're spending a dollar, you want some multiple of that dollar to come back in the form of sales. Let's just for the sake of argument say that your ROAS target is 5x. When you spend a dollar, you're expecting to get $5 in sales back. That's not $5 in profit, it's $5 in sales. That's a fairly low ROAS, but you can understand the thinking here, that we want to think like an investor. We have to imagine that when we're paying for traffic, we are paying for a portfolio of assets. One of those assets might be a Google search campaign with around a very specific set of keywords. That's an investment. That investment has a certain amount of money we can spend. Let's say that we can spend $50 a day to buy that high quality traffic. We have a certain return that we expect from that. This is important later because if we only have $50 a day to spend, we don't want to distribute that across one campaign that does really well and then another that does okay and then a third that we're not really sure about. You'd never do that with investments. You would put all of your money where it's most likely to generate the highest return. That's what we want to do with paid traffic as well. You'll see as I go through the various frameworks to do that, I'm always thinking like an investor to make sure that I'm spending my money where it's most likely to create a high positive return and if there's more money to be spent, especially when we get into topics of scale, when there's more money to spend, I still want to use that same framework. I don't want to just spend. I don't want to skip steps because I'm not willing to do the work to figure out what's generating returns and what isn't. Next Andre and I talk about this all the time. We optimize for the system optimum. We don't optimize for local optima. What does that mean in English? It means I don't really care at the end of the day what cost per lead is. I don't care about the numbers within the system in so far as though I don't want to optimize those numbers because if I get focused myopically on a part of the system and try to make that part that part better, I might make the whole system worse. An example I use a lot in the traffic engine is optimizing for lead cost to try to drive lead cost down is very easy. I can make a lead generating machine that at the very same time undermines my overall goal of generating happy customers. So when I optimize for the system optimum, that means I need to go back to step one here and know my goals with precision and clarity. If my goal is to increase my number of happy customers, that's always what I'm making each optimization decision based on. Will this increase the number of happy customers? I'm not asking will this increase leads? Will this drive my quality score up? Those questions are irrelevant unless they directly affect the overall goal, the system optimum of generating happy customers. This one seems obvious, but this is probably one of the things I've seen happen more often than anything else in my career. So what's magical thinking? Magical thinking is when I see this manifest in many different ways, but it's always some form of a narrative that somebody has created that involves four or five steps that when you chain them all together, some result is going to happen. Or it's a way to explain something that's already happening by making dozens or at least over five intellectual leaps of faith. I'll talk about some specific examples of this later when I mentioned where magical thinking tends to show up a lot. Often in my experience, magical thinking is a way to avoid understanding how something works. It's a way to say, well, I'm going to buy some traffic for my podcast and then people are going to listen to it and when they listen to it, they're going to want my thing and when they want my thing, they're going to go over here and then they're going to do this and then when that happens and then everything's going to be better because of my campaign for my podcast. Maybe that's true. Maybe that's absolutely true, but there are a lot of steps in there. There are a lot of things that need to happen and I don't know that we can verify many of those steps. So rather than magical thinking, what I want to do is just look at the phrase I use a lot is, is it intellectually defensible? And part of that is can I draw it with a crayon? I sit down and I show you the A to B to C to D causality of how I think something works. Or, and again, I understand that it's not always A to B to C to D causal in a systems view, but systems don't create magic. And sometimes they feel that way. But systems create a result because of the relationships among the parts. That's not magical thinking. Magical thinking is when you don't really know how something works. So you tell yourself a story about either how you'd like it to work or how it could possibly work and then make decisions based on that. We just don't want to do that. Later in this presentation, I'll give more specific examples and I think it'll make it a little bit clearer. And the last element here about thinking like an investor is all decisions create opportunity costs. This is so important. This is probably the most important thing that you'll learn in this module. Maybe one of the most important things that you'll learn in the traffic engine in total, every time we choose to do one thing, we say yes to one thing. We're saying no to others. So let's use the same example I mentioned earlier. Let's assume I have $50 a day to spend on paid traffic. And I just, and I know that yes, there's a bullseye ad words campaign that's really, really good. And, you know, I've decided I want to spend $10 on that campaign. Now I could spend all 50, but I also want to do something on Facebook because I've heard Facebook is pretty cool too. And I feel like I should be, I should spread things around. Right. So I spend $10 on a really good campaign on Google ads. I don't spend the next 40 on it. Instead, I put that next 40 into some other things. That's another ad words campaign that may not be as good, but for whatever reason, I think it's, it's, it can help. Maybe it's a top of funnel campaign. And then I put the rest of it into Facebook. Does that mean that the Facebook campaigns and the retargeting campaigns and the other ad words campaigns are bad ideas? No. But what it does mean is that the cost of doing those things and not putting the money into a place that is ultra high value creates an opportunity cost that if I can spend that $50 on a really good campaign and I don't, I've, I've created a cost for myself. This happens a lot. This particularly happens if you are a service provider, if you run an agency and there are competing priorities within a business or a client where one team wants X and then somebody else wants, has this idea about this other thing. And, and everybody gets a little piece of the marketing budget. And what happens at the end of the day is the results are kind of muddy or suboptimal because they're being spread around to make other, to make sure everybody's sort of happy with how the ad spend has been distributed. Don't make this mistake. I've seen that happen so many times. I've seen so many campaigns that when we trace back to what the problem was, it was the problem was the somebody tried to be all things to everybody and you can't, you just can't do it. And if your service provider, this is a very difficult issue to deal with because often it's the boss. The boss says, it doesn't, but the boss says I have X amount of money to spend each month and you're spending the money the way that you think is the best distributed, the best ways. Yet there's this other campaign he or she wants to do. And then there's this other thing that they want to do. And this other thing. And before you know it, half of your budget is going to things that you don't, that the money being spent there when viewed on its own is probably okay. But when it's viewed in from the lens of what you're not doing because you're spending the money there, it's not okay. All decisions create opportunity costs and you have to live with those costs. Many of you for budgetary reasons, going through the traffic engine, certainly the first time, maybe the second time through, maybe even the third time through, shouldn't spend any money on anything other than your bull's eye keywords. Right. And I get, I get how that might feel a little awkward. Like, well, I paid for this course. It covers lots of topics. I feel like I should be doing these other things. But if, if doing other things like retargeting or Facebook is at the expense of doing the one thing that's likely to generate the highest possible return for your investment, you shouldn't do those other things, plain and simple. So I will put that into some context and how to think about that as we go forward through this presentation. But opportunity cost is, is, it's such an important factor. And this is something that I really want you to internalize and embrace. Every time you say yes to something, take a moment and ask yourself what saying yes to that something means you have to say no to. Now, this is certainly applicable beyond paid traffic, but for this conversation, we'll keep it focused on paid traffic. All right, let's move on. So I want to give you the 30,000 foot view. This is the roadmap view of how to think about paid traffic. Now that you've gone through the entire course, this is how to, to really look back on the course and to look forward to how to think about in broad terms where you want to focus your energy, your attention in your ad spend. So first and foremost, Google ads search. That's active intent. I go through why that's so important. I've been one of the earlier modules about Google ads. In particular, you want to focus on bull's eye keywords. And this is where I'll take a moment. I mean, I'll give you the back history of that because I, and that's where this entire metaphor comes from for this entire presentation. Back in the early 2000s, the term bull's eye keywords was created by a gentleman named Glenn Livingston. Glenn Livingston actually lives close to me in New Hampshire. I've never met him personally, but he was a very significant intellectual influence on my, the way I thought about paid traffic early. And his, and this was back when traffic was cheap. But what Glenn imagined was that there was a spectrum of keywords, that, that spectrum of keywords indicated intent. And the higher level, the intent, the more valuable the keyword and that within any audience, there was a set of bull's eye keywords. These, those were the things that were so applicable to your business that you, that they were slam dunks. You absolutely needed to pay for that traffic first. So that's where everything starts. And if you can, if you, your budget runs out, purchasing bull's eye keywords, don't do anything else, nothing else, just purchase those first. After that, what we call high value keywords. I'll explain the distinction between a bull's eye keyword and a high value keyword later when we focus specifically on Google ads. But again, if, if you can't, this is where opportunity costs. Comes in that if you can't pay for all of the bull's eye keywords first, don't go looking for the higher value ones. Don't move outside the center of the bull's eye until you have, you have to. It's not like, well, I feel like I should, I feel like I should cover the, the entire, the top, the middle, the bottom, the funnel. That's not true. Your goal as an investor is to put your money where it's likely to generate the highest return period. That's your job. And bull's eye keywords with active search intent are the most likely to create a positive return period. Next up, after I've set up these, after I've, and again, exhausted budget. So I've spent all the money I can on bull's eye keywords. I've spent money on high value keywords. I've exhausted those opportunities. There's no more there, there. That's the point at which I would, I would start thinking about retargeting. And I would retire, I'll retarget across both platforms, Google ads and Facebook simultaneously. A few things to think about for retargeting. First and foremost, use quality indicators. So somebody needs to stay on the page or your site for a certain period of time. They might need to see a certain number of pages. They might need to show with their behavior that there's, there are quality prospect. Certainly emphasizing bottom and middle of the funnel goals first is very important. So what does that mean? Bottom of the funnel is when someone's about to become a customer. On that last step from really highly qualified prospect to becoming a customer. That's where you want to focus your retargeting dollars first. It's a small pool of traffic in general, relatively small pool of traffic, but ultra high quality and ultra high ROI. And then middle of the funnel, you know, that's often the, trying to get someone to go from awareness to engagement and with qualitative indicators. Maybe they didn't opt in, for example, but they stayed on the page for a long period of time. And they've shown you that they're interested. They just didn't take that next step. So that's where I would focus retargeting at this 30,000 foot view after Google ad search. Next would be Facebook campaigns. You know, a couple of rules to think about with the Facebook rules, a couple of suggestions with Facebook campaigns. We'll get into, we're going to get into each of these specifics later in this presentation. But for now at this high level for Facebook campaigns, a couple of things to think about. Give Facebook algorithm lots of room. You give it a big audiences. And we'll go into the specifics, but we want Facebook, we want to use Facebook's algorithm on our behalf. So we want it to be able to go find customers, use it, black box data to find our customers and our prospects. And we really want to focus a lot of energy and attention on testing offers and testing value propositions. That's, those are ultra high leverage. And again, I'll explain that in a little bit. Before I go to the last part here, I do want to jump up and talk about retargeting. This, this presentation is being recorded in early March, 2021. Last week, Google just announced that it's going to, if I'm re, if I'm understanding what they're saying correctly, they're going to get rid of individual level retargeting. So once we have a clear of, in this hat, this is going to happen at some point in the future, it's part of the larger issues around privacy. I have not heard a straight answer. I, I'm very surprised to imagine that Google will completely eliminate retargeting at the individual level. I can certainly understand why they might be just discussing that as a possibility, but I'm still very surprised to imagine that they would eliminate that entirely. Knowing that once we get closer to better understanding what the full ramifications of Google's decisions are, I'll update this presentation. And we'll talk about where retargeting fits, fits in. It's entirely possible. Retargeting at the individual level is just going to go away. That seems to be what the major platforms are suggesting. And if that's the case, then we'll have to think about this an entirely different way. Moving on to Facebook campaigns. And again, I want, I want to draw your attention here to notice how we're moving out from the center of the bull's eye at each of these, as we're talking about this 30,000 foot view, like the bull, the center of the bull's eye as Google search, particular type of search, the high value in the bull's eye keywords. When we move out, still very valuable is retargeting at bottom and middle of the funnel. If we have the right quality indicators in place, when we move a little farther out, this is where Facebook, and I think, you know, I might move Facebook out into the black and or ish, you know, it's hard. It's hard to know. Facebook works sometimes. It doesn't work as well other times. It depends on what budget you're working with. It depends on your audiences. It depends on your, certainly your offer. So these are broad strokes. Just consider this broad strokes. And then the last, again, 30,000 foot view, we move a little bit farther away. If there's still budget to spend, then I would use that budget at top of funnel for both Google ads and Facebook. These are general search terms that usually indicates that sort of an early stage of search or very low awareness, so not nearly as valuable, often far less expensive. In Facebook, broadly speaking, this could be awareness building content, videos, podcasts, educational content, anything that makes an audience aware, but is not asking them to do something in return. Retargeting is where we start to move them closer to you. All right, let's get into specifics for the platforms. This is how I think about optimization and scale for Google ads search. First thing, talked about this over and over already, bull's eye keywords. Right, this is that's where the term came from. That's where the metaphor came from. Bull's eye keywords are those things that are so clear. Like when somebody searches for that phrase, it is so clear that they're a good prospect for you. The example I use in the traffic engine, I use it a lot, is if you sell red widgets online and you offer free shipping and somebody searches for buy red widgets online free shipping, that's a bull's eye keyword. It's perfect, perfect match. Bull's eye keywords are there's art in science. Right. Science generally happens over time when we see the data. The art is to is to think about it through the lens of the prospect, what he or she might be looking for that really, really makes it perfect for you. Sometimes the subtleties here are very significant. Sometimes the keyword phrase that should work doesn't because you have a competitor that's just really well known. So it's not you, it's the context. Other times there are things that you're with your value proposition that there's a problem. It's just, it's really, you have to balance all of those things, but it's so worth it. We need to focus here first, that we need to exhaust this first because your time and your energy and your dollars spent here pay significant dividends. This is where the money is to borrow Willie Sutton's phrase. If you're not familiar with Willie Sutton, he was a bank robber and the judge asked him, Willie, why do you rob banks? He said, because that's where the money is bull's eye keywords. That's where the money is. If you are, if you're struggling to get your bull's eye keywords to work and you think, I'm just going to go do Facebook instead. That is an enormous opportunity cost. Your time spent dialing in bull's eye keywords is time well spent. If you exhaust your budget and you cannot buy more, then we move on to sort of this next ring, which are these high value keywords. Again, this is, this is not an exact science. It's not like this one's, the data will tell you which ones truly are bull's eye versus which ones are not. The thing to pay very close attention to is the monetization. What is your ROI? If you're doing e-commerce, what's your ROAS? If you're, if you're, if it's more of a direct response model, and what's your CPA relative to your AOV and what's LTV look like? You have to look at those because sometimes a high value keyword might have borderline, like breakeven or slightly negative AOV relative to CPA. So it might, you might have a bull's eye keyword that when you, when you pay for the keyword, you're making 5, 10, 15 X on the first sale. And a high value keyword might be your breakeven on the first sale, but lifetime over six plus months makes it ROI positive. That, that can be the distinction. So if you're borderline with the monetization, you have to pay very close attention to them because if you just kind of let it go for a while and it's, it moves from borderline to negative, then it can become a problem. So testing is absolutely critical. A distinction between a bull's eye keyword and a high value keyword, this is a, this is a general way to think about it. But I would consider in the example that I gave above, buy red widgets online, free shipping, absolute bull's eye keyword. If I sell red widgets online and offer free shipping, I would consider buy red widgets to be high value. There's purchase intent in there. Somebody wants to buy the thing that I sell. Now I don't know if they want, if they're willing to buy it online, but they're searching online. So I'm going to infer they might, and they didn't specify free shipping, but maybe free shipping doesn't matter to them. That's the kind of thinking that we use here. Again, art and science, and then let the data tell us, we have to always be looking at the data to confirm or disconfirm what we think is going on. And if you've exhausted all on the ad work, on the Google ad side, once you've gone through, there's no, there are no more bull's eye keywords to buy. You've done the optimization there. You're focused on that. You're making the right improvements there. You've done the same with high value. Then we can start thinking about what I consider top of funnel or audience builder keywords. These are usually general terms, for example, red widgets. They generally indicate an early stage of search, and or often low awareness. Like the prospect doesn't really know what he or she is looking for. They're just starting out their search. Much higher volume, which makes them really tempted. He's looking to say, wow, why am I spending all of this money on these bull's eye keywords? They're so expensive and there just aren't a lot of them. But wow, look at those other terms. There's so much volume and they're less expensive. Right. Because they're worthless. Again, they're not worthless. They're worthless. And if we're thinking like an investor, we put our money first in the bull's eye keywords to get the highest return, even though they're more expensive. Then we put our money in the high value keywords. But we're still getting, we think good positive return there. And then we invest in top of funnel and then we look at it very carefully. Okay. This is where we have to be really cautious. One area we have to be really cautious about magical thinking. It's easy to construct a narrative. And it's, the narrative often sounds like, you know, if X then Y then Z. And you realize you're doing some magical thinking. So if somebody searches for red widgets and then they come to my site and they see that we sell red widgets online and that they could, and that we have free shipping too, then they're just going to want to buy them. Really? Does that make sense? Because there are a lot of assumptions in there. That's magical thinking. It might be true. Some percentage of people might search for red widgets because they have buying intent and they find you and because they found you, they go and they look and think, you know what, this is the time for me to buy red widgets. Perfect. I'll buy them from these guys because they're, you know, not only are they seem like they're great, but they offer free shipping. Maybe. But that's, there's some magical thinking in there. So this is, this is the high level framework for Google ads search. All right. Let's move on to retargeting. And this is cross-platform retargeting. And again, this may be going away. I'm still, I'm stunned by this. I've read and reread Google's announcements. I understand. I know what the words mean. Yet I'm just struggling to believe they mean what I think they mean. So again, if retargeting goes away, this will be updated. But for now and certainly in the near future, this is how I would approach retargeting. First, we start at the center of the bull's eye's bottom of the funnel. We're taking highly engaged prospects and we're trying to move them that last step to becoming customers. An e-commerce space, this could be someone who's looked at a specific product and you're retargeting with that product. Then they've spent a certain amount of time on that page or certain category of products. Or if it's a, if you offer a course or if you just put an offer out into the world and people have really engaged with that content, but they haven't gone that last step, they haven't actually become a customer, that's bottom of the funnel. That's where I would focus my attention. What does it take to get somebody who has shown through their behavior that they're already really engaged to then become a customer? Somebody to think about our testing, this is really true across much of what we do, but testing mediums, video versus text, if you showed somebody text and they're right on the precipice there, but they just haven't quite made it from prospect to customer, but retarget with video, test the two against each other. Some people are more visual, some people want to see an infographic, some people want to watch a short video, some people want to read text. You just have to play with this and test all of those. And it's not necessarily either or. Oftentimes it's yes to all of these options. You want to have all of those in your toolbox to find the different parts of the audience that, who resonate with different mediums. And testing value propositions also very important, sort of how you, how you present your, your offer and why it's valuable and the language that you use. So you can think of this as testing value proposition and offers, but I don't mean when I say testing offers that at this bottom of the funnel stage, the offer already exists. So it's really about testing the, the expression and what you're focusing on in the value proposition. So you have a little room to do that. This work is very high ROI. It's low volume relative to the other traffic that you can buy, but it's high ROI. It's worth spending time here. Next up, middle of the funnel. These are, this is an engaged audience again through their behavior and other indicators. These are usually people right before they become leads. And again, very similar to how we think about bottom of the funnel. You want to test mediums, video versus text versus images and other things. You want to test value propositions. We're trying to move somebody from, they seem really interested in what we're doing. They just haven't taken that next step to become a lead. One word of warning here. This is where we get back to that point about local optima versus the system optima. Don't get too crazy about optimizing for leads. Leads, there's a, there's a whole body of thought around lead gen. And I've seen this happen so many times and I'm very, very cautious about it. And I'm very sensitive to it, which is you can learn how to generate leads really, really well. And those leads have no relationship that the volume and the cost of those leads have no relationship to the number of customers that you generate. That seems odd because becoming a lead is a precursor to becoming a customer in many business models, but a lead is not a customer. And you can, when you start thinking about optimizing for leads, it changes the thinking, it, it, it, it just, everything changes and it can, it can become very myopic, very fast. And you, and it's, and it's intoxicating. When you, one day you're, you're, you're spending $10 for a lead and the next it's seven and then it's five and then it's three. And then all of a sudden you're getting a lot of leads or you're building a big list. That can become self perpetuating. And it, and it's often a, the real indicators of what are going to happen are lagging. Right. We think the lead is a lead, generating leads is a leading indicator, which means more leads will mean more customers later. That's not always true. Often it's not true. So what we care about is that, how do we assess the quality of those leads? So because what the business that we're in is generating happy customers, not generating leads. So I don't want to beat this to death. But just, just being very cautious about that. That's a mistake I see made so often that I want you to be aware of it. And then for retargeting, we move on to top of funnel. Educational content, this is using, this is educational content to build awareness, to establish positioning. Again, be cautious about having this, you know, sort of weird narrative where it's like, well, if I do this and then I do this and then this and then unicorns come and then this happens, and then I'm going to have this magical thing happen. I think I would even be tempted to remove top of funnel from here entirely. And again, we don't know the state of retargeting. For now, I think we can make a case that, yes, we want to have some top of funnel content that if someone visits our site, they haven't become a lead, they haven't become a customer, they're not really close to us yet, but they've done something. They've stayed, they've seen a couple pages, they've looked at some of our stuff, that we could show more educational content. We could continue, we could just have our stuff in their Facebook feed, for example, or they could see our ads other places, sure. That makes sense, but at the same time, opportunity cost is the issue here. As long as you're doing that when you've done all of the other things first, makes sense, but don't spend any time here if you haven't done everything else before this first. All right, this is the harder one. Part of the struggle I had with this module, actually, was that the framework I originally envisioned tried to have a single way to look at Google and Facebook side by side on a single metaphor with a bull's eye. And that was really hard because they're not the same thing. You know, I harp on this in the traffic engine that Facebook is passive potential interest. People are not actively looking for your stuff. It's a combination of psychographic and demographic and other factors that we hope, an interest in particular, that we hope indicate that that person might be a decent prospect, and we're relying on Facebook's algorithm to really put all those pieces together. That's a lot different than somebody raising his or her hand and saying, I'm looking for X in UcelX. They're just so fundamentally different that they're hard to superimpose on one another over the same sort of metaphor. I did that earlier and try to show sort of the order, but it is still continues to surprise me how quickly we go to Facebook without first looking at Google. It doesn't mean that Facebook isn't sometimes the best option. Facebook fills a very specific need. If somebody's not actively looking or not actively looking at scale, Facebook can be a place where we can really scale. Let's, what I want to give you now is sort of a framework to think about, how to think about scaling from the Facebook perspective. First and foremost, and I owe a debt of gratitude for Lauren for helping me with this model too, because I had the model that I originally had before I got Lauren's input wasn't as good. It wasn't as accurate in conversation with Lauren is what really, if this is really good, it's because of Lauren's input. If this is not really good, then the fault lies entirely with me, but I feel a lot better about this. This represents reality for me better than any framework I've seen or I've created. At the center of the bull's eye for Facebook is not anything technical. It's the offer in the ad, particularly the offer. We say this a lot internally. We've said this internally in the past on the agency side, that if we have a really good offer, everything else is easy. If we have a really bad offer, nothing else is easy. At the beginning and the end for Facebook is you need an offer that resonates with the audience. And related to the offer is the ad, because the ad is the thing that gets the attention of somebody so that they are even aware of the offer, because they're not looking for it. The offer is the most important factor and you need to test offers. I can't stress that enough and so few people do that. But to be clear, you can't test offers every 72 hours. You need to have, and this is a mistake we've seen happen a lot, you need to have an offer that you think makes sense. Or you can use the framework, the testing framework is another way to start this, but you need to get something that where the offer is the most important factor, the offer has enough opportunity to get some data. So if you're doing a webinar, for example, and the first webinar doesn't work, don't switch topics for the next webinar. You've not done not nearly enough work to know if the offer isn't going to convert. You need to give that more time. So it's not a matter of just throw up an offer, see what happens, throw up another offer, see what happens. It's not that. It's start with an offer, get some data. It may take you a month. It may be you do a webinar, for example, and the webinar presents the offer at the end. And the first few times you run the webinar, you spend $1,000 and you get $600 or $700 in return, or $500, whatever it is. You have to make an educated decision. What do you think is going on here? You sort of look at it. You can look at when people dropped off. You could look at a lot of factors. And I can't imagine all of those for you, but you need to look at all of that and say, okay, let's make an educated guess to make this offer a little bit better. What do we think needs to happen? And let's say for the sake of argument that it's an offer that is delivered once a week at a certain time slot and your show up rate was really bad, meaning you've got 100 people to sign up and 10 showed up. Well, that's the problem to solve immediately. So how do you get people to show up more? It's not, hey, let's try a different offer and see if that different offer gets more than 10 people to show up. Like that's, I'm not explaining this well, because this is one of those things that you really have to, you really have to dig in and look at lots of data. But at some point, it might be three weeks, five weeks, but at some point, you're going to have done the work to understand if the offer has a chance. Does it have legs? If you're break even with an offer, then keep tweaking the offer. Don't reinvent a new offer. But if you've presented the offer to 50, 600, 200 people and no one's really taking it, then you need to really wonder, is this the right offer? If it's a low cost offer, which for me, I don't think there's a hard and fast rule about a low cost offer. But for me, I would say it's $97, $97 or less, for a low cost offer in my mind. If you have a low cost offer and nobody's buying it, you don't need to send a thousand people to see the offer before you realize nobody's buying it. It might be a hundred. Maybe I probably want to do 250 and say, okay, so 250 people, the targeting looks good. Like there's all these, you know, plenty of indicators of people, they were on the page for a long time, whatever it is, but they, no one really bought it. Okay, I don't think the offer is that great. Let's test a different offer. So it really depends on the scope and the scale of what you're testing for, how long and how much data that you need to get. So if you're doing a webinar, you need to plan on more time, longer durations, more ad spend. That's another critical factor. You can test a lot more and a lot faster with larger ad spend. If you have less money to spend and you want to test offers, then test lower cost offers, because you'll get more information faster. I mean, you'll have a more likely you'll get return as well. And then when you're thinking about ads, you really want an ad that accurately expresses the value proposition. That's critically important. The copy needs to be clear. You know, it's don't rely on curiosity and intrigue to get clicks. You really, you want the value proposition to be expressed well in the ad. And the center of the bullseye is testing high leverage ad elements, especially things like the hook, the first two lines. Images are certainly they're part of that. But it's it's really I like testing the hook because that those first two lines are really what's going to grab somebody's attention. That's a high leverage thing to test. All right. Second, well, this is in I could I could argue this is closer to the center of the bullseye to let Facebook do the work for you. This is different than I would have said three or four years ago. The Facebook algorithm is very smart. They have lots of data, 400,000 data points. They understand the behavior of people. So when you give Facebook and Lauren explained this very well in the trap in the specific modules, when you give Facebook a really large audience, you're saying you're giving them a big pool of people to go find your prospects or your customers in and the algorithm can work on your behalf. You also need to give them meaningful conversion events. If you give Facebook a lead conversion event, they'll find people who sign up for things and give their email for them, not necessarily customers. So if you can use a conversion event that's most meaningful, which would be would be a sale, even if it's a low cost initial sale, not like a $7 sale. But you know, unless that works for your business model, but something that indicates that this is a higher quality prospect. If you make that the conversion event and you give Facebook a large audience, then you are you're giving Facebook the data it needs, it needs to use its data on your behalf. And then, you know, focus on bottom middle of the funnel campaigns. You know, we want to start our attention on turning people into customers. That's bottom of the funnel. And we also want to focus our attention after that, after converting people from prospect to customer, we want to create people from awareness to engagement in the form of a lead. I would do those things first before you do the other stuff, right? That's that's this is this is very important from a framework perspective. Start to see it initially start to see if you can create customers from Facebook. Just pay attention to that. If you can create customers immediately, especially from cold audiences, don't don't try to do a bunch of extra steps. Just make customers. There are some if you have a highly an offer that converts, you may be able to make customers right out of the gate and that's excellent. Don't go try to generate leads if you can generate customers. If that's a little bit harder for your business model or other things, and then try to generate leads before you just try to generate awareness. That's really again, this is opportunity cost. The cost of generating leads at the expense of generating customers is pretty clear. You wouldn't if I gave you a choice, would you rather spend $1,000 to get $1,000 worth of customers? Or would you rather or $1,000 worth of sales? Or would you rather spend $1,000 and get 100 leads? I'll take the $1,000 in customers right in sales makes more sense. One thing to be cautious of is pounding your the middle and bottom of the funnel and in leading to audience fatigue. This actually often happens when something works and then you just hammer it. This is really working. Let's just turn the volume up on this as high as you can. And then you spend a ton of money and you burn out an audience pretty quickly. So be cautious of that. The solution to audience fatigue is to create other offers or different expressions of the offers. Like that's one effective solution for dealing with audience fatigue. And as we get farther out, we got to feed the middle of the funnel. So we got to create top of funnel campaigns to create warmer audiences that we can turn those warmer audiences at. We have a better chance to turn them into customers and turn them into leads. This is another opportunity to test mediums. Your audience may respond and maybe your product lends itself more to video versus text. Who knows? Or images or whatever you test this stuff to. But don't test video versus text at a top of the funnel campaign before you've done all these other things, before you've thought about these other things. This is really the framework for thinking about Facebook. And what I say is the framework, what I mean is any amount of mental energy you spend thinking about your next really great top of funnel campaign that you don't spend instead on tweaking offers is a tremendously wasted opportunity cost. Terrible. Because it doesn't mean that all day, every day is just thinking about offers and ads. But that's a big part of the day. That's a big part of what scale is all about is to sit down and think, okay, we've done, we're checking all the boxes. We've got lots of campaigns that are working, all kinds of things are making sense. But I know we got to come up with the next offer. We got to come up with the next thing. Like that's high value time. But if you say instead, like I'm just focusing on offers, I don't know if that's as important. You know, what's feed the middle of the funnel by creating some top of funnel campaigns? Well, you can. But that it's unlikely that's the best decision for the use of your time, especially if you're a small operation, if it's just you, every decision you make has a cost. And what this this framework here is really trying to show is that the cost of the outer rings of the circle are at the expense of the things in the inner rings of the circle. Now, you may have spent two or three weeks on offers and ads, and you've got all these ideas, and you've put things in place, and they're running. At that point, then sure, think about your middle of the funnel campaign, give yourself a break, or you're talking, feed, feed, think about top of the funnel campaigns that to to generate warmer audiences, for example, right, because you've already done the work toward the center of the bull's eye. But don't don't make these less value activities, the majority of your work, like spend your time where it's likely this gets back to this central idea of thinking, like an investor, you want to spend your time where it's most likely to create the highest possible positive return on your time and your energy and your dollars. Like that is that is the name of the game and returning full circle to why paid traffic to the original value proposition of the traffic engine. It really is that same idea. Yes, we could do organic search, we could do LinkedIn, we could do lots of things, but to do those things would come at a cost of not doing paid traffic on Google and Facebook. And from my experience in my perspective, that trade off isn't worth it. The problem comes down to asking a question. This is a question I've heard asked, or it's an implication I've heard in more client board rooms and conference rooms and over tables and over zoom that I cared account, which is the difference between could and should. The most people ask the question, is this something that we could do? And could when you ask that question, then you're analyzing the activity on its own merits. Like is this, does this make sense? Right? Like, does this, you know, is this a good idea? Right? And it assumes that time, energy and dollars are infinite. So sure, you could do it. But that's not reality. The reality is time, energy and dollars are finite. So the quite the better question is with the resources we have in time, which if you're a solopreneur is your time, and the resources we have in time and in energy and in dollars to spend, what should we do? And this entire now 51 minute presentation is really designed to give you the frameworks to answer that question for yourself to think about, I have X amount of time to build my business with paid traffic over the course of a day, a week, a month, a year, whatever it is. And knowing that, here's how I'm going to focus my energy and my attention and my ad spend. And each of these frameworks answers that for the high level, and then at the individual level, so that once you use the high level framework to orient yourself, then you have a framework within that framework to know how to focus your attention, because the reality is we can't do everything. There are so many parts about internet marketing that I don't know anything about, because I just don't have the bandwidth to do them all. So I've tried to organize my thinking around those things that produce the highest return. I'll wrap up almost an hour, which is a surprise. I didn't think we'd go that long. I'll wrap up with this question. What's next? And there's really what's next for you. And then there's what's next for the traffic engine. Let's start with what's next for you. I think going through the traffic engine beginning to end one time is a good idea. Doing the work along the way. I would like you all to have a bullseye search campaign, a retargeting campaign on Google ads and Google display network and Facebook, and a Facebook campaign. I'd like to have built one of those and have each of those and just have a sense of the flavor of each of those. Then you can go back and decide what you want to do next, following the outlines in this module. I think if you can get more, and again, it's all spelled out, but if you're in a market where there's more search traffic, go do searching, do search exceptionally well, and just follow the framework and keep building those bullseye search campaigns, and maybe some of the high value search campaigns. And that might take weeks, that might take months, it might take the rest of the year, and that's perfectly okay because that's high value work. You're getting a high return. Your business may not work as well for search. That's true for lots of businesses. It's hard to scale and search because there's just not a lot of search volume, and that moves you over toward Facebook. That's why the traffic engine includes both. They pair very well together. So if that's you, then focus more of your energy and attention on Facebook, and then just build through the campaigns and the order that I've described in this module. Continue to go back to the content for the traffic engine because every time you do something, you'll see the content a little bit differently. So if Facebook is your thing, focus on the Facebook modules. I would say at least once, maybe twice a year, read the first couple of modules again, just as a refresher, or as a reorient yourself, module zero is one and two, just to refresh yourself. So that's how I would approach it if I were you. Now let's talk about what's next for the traffic engine. Paid traffic is a dynamic living thing. It's changing incredibly rapidly. I mentioned earlier, this is March 2021, so we're well in the thick of dealing with the Apple Facebook issue with iOS 14. That's going to continue to be a very significant issue around privacy that has massive reverberations through the paid traffic world. Now Google's announcement, same seismic announcement. And the world is shifting to wanting more privacy. So that's going to have ramifications, but paid traffic is not going away. So what's next for the traffic engine is I'm going to go back to the beginning. I've done this a couple of times already just polishing things, but now it's a matter of going back to the beginning and determining how I can explain each thing better and faster and more effectively for each student who goes through it. And that I don't know if we'll do the traffic engine as releases. I don't know if there'll be you know, TTE2. We are going to do that with with Sphere of Influence, version two will be out this year. Autoresponder Madness is on version four and we're changing the name for those. I don't know if we'll do that with the traffic engine. My my intuition is that the traffic engine will just be a living course where it's always as up to date as it can be. And the last thing that I've thought about a lot is what do we add to make it even better? I've mentioned in some of the Q&A calls that I think it's probably worth our time to add LinkedIn. It may be worth adding something on YouTube as well. YouTube has been a little bit of the Wild West lately because of Facebook. And some of the issues with Facebook, a lot of advertisers have flocked to YouTube. YouTube is its own animal and my fear and the reason I didn't include YouTube in the traffic engine in the beginning is that every YouTube story I've heard from big advertisers over the past say 12 to 15 years is some variation of this. We wanted to make money on YouTube. We had no idea what we were doing. We ended up spending half a million dollars before we figured it out. That's half a million plus. That's really, it sounds crazy, but that's the variation of story I've heard so many times about YouTube. So YouTube is one of those things that's very high volume, low quality, and it takes a certain mentality and it takes a certain budget that doesn't make it a good fit for our audience. So I'm not ruling YouTube out, but I also don't ever want to put our students in a position where they could cost themselves a lot of money and create a lot of problems as part of the learning curve. I'm not really comfortable with that. You can do Google Well, you can do Facebook Well, you can do those with smaller budgets and less risk. And I lean toward that. I've leaned toward that for decades with my clients. I lean toward that for myself and I'm very protective of our students and I lean that way for you too. So not ruling it out, not quite willing to rule it in. It has been an absolute pleasure to spend this last almost hour with all of you. I appreciate, as always, your attention and I'm looking forward to what's next for the traffic engine.
